pathophysiology_summary: Viral gastroenteritis (norovirus/rotavirus) is a self-limiting infection causing sudden watery, non-bloody diarrhoea, nausea/vomiting, crampy abdominal pain and malaise, resolving over a few days; the main risk is dehydration/electrolyte loss. It is benign. The ground-truth task is to (1) exclude a surgical abdomen (appendicitis — migratory/localised RLQ pain, peritonism; bowel obstruction — colicky pain with vomiting, no flatus/stool, distension) and invasive/bacterial enterocolitis (blood/mucus in stool, high sustained fever, toxicity, travel), (2) assess and manage dehydration with oral rehydration solution given as small, frequent sips (± an antiemetic to break the vomiting cycle), and (3) AVOID routine antidiarrhoeals (loperamide can worsen invasive infection) and antibiotics (not indicated for viral gastroenteritis), with hygiene to prevent spread.

differential.evidence_basis: Sudden watery non-bloody diarrhoea + vomiting + crampy pain + malaise.
Screen severe/localised RLQ or constant pain, peritonism, no flatus/stool with distension.
Screen blood/mucus, high sustained fever/toxicity, travel — may need stool tests/antibiotics.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Viral gastroenteritis may lead, but a surgical abdomen and invasive/bacterial diarrhoea must be screened before conservative rehydration.

differential.evidence_basis: Crampy (eases after stool) non-localised pain, still passing diarrhoea, no blood/mucus/high fever/travel — viral gastroenteritis; dehydration risk from vomiting.
No localised/constant peritonitic pain or obstruction features; escalation target if they develop.
No blood/mucus/high sustained fever/travel; escalation target if they develop.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a negative surgical/invasive screen, viral gastroenteritis leads; oral rehydration ± antiemetic, avoiding antidiarrhoeals/antibiotics, with dehydration and surgical safety-nets.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis; vomiting settles in ~24h, diarrhoea over a few days; supportive care.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final position is benign viral gastroenteritis with a dehydration focus.

red_flags.description: Surgical abdomen — severe constant or localised (e.g. right lower quadrant) pain, a rigid/guarded/rebound-tender abdomen, or obstruction (colicky pain, vomiting, no flatus/stool, distension)
Invasive/bacterial diarrhoea or dehydration — blood/mucus in stool, high sustained fever/toxicity, recent travel; or unable to keep fluids down, reduced urine output, marked lightheadedness
red_flags.clinical_significance: May need stool tests/antibiotics; dehydration may need ED/IV fluids.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Attributing all pain/vomiting to gastroenteritis without screening for a localising/constant surgical abdomen.
Loperamide can worsen invasive infection; antibiotics are not indicated for viral gastroenteritis.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Oral rehydration (small frequent sips) ± antiemetic; ED for a surgical abdomen or failed rehydration; GP/stool tests for blood/high fever/travel features.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Manage acute gastroenteritis with oral rehydration solution (small, frequent amounts); an antiemetic (e.g. ondansetron) can help vomiting. Avoid routine antidiarrhoeals and antibiotics. Investigate/escalate for blood in stool, high fever, dehydration, or features of a surgical abdomen.
